exports.content: CIB accuracy degrades slightly (≤1.4 percentage points) when scaling the prior model from 1.5B to 7B parameters without re-tuning the $\beta$ hyperparameter. This indicates that $\beta$ values are not transferable across prior model sizes and must be re-calibrated when using a different prior.
exports.label: accuracy_degradation_at_scale

exports.content: Standard Information Bottleneck (IB) assumes a Markov chain structure Y ↔ X ↔ Z, where the compressed representation Z mediates all information from input X to output Y. However, transformer decoder architectures violate this Markov assumption: via causal self-attention and the key-value cache, the answer generation directly attends to both the prompt X and reasoning trace Z simultaneously. This creates a collider structure (X,Z)→Y rather than the chain X→Z→Y. The authors term this inconsistency the 'Attention Paradox'.
exports.label: attention_paradox

exports.content: CIB with semantic prior achieves a superior Pareto frontier over L1-based length penalties on DLER-7B: at similar compression levels (~29-32%), CIB $\beta^+$ achieves 52.9% average accuracy (−1.3pp from baseline) versus L1-Exact's 51.5% (−2.7pp from baseline). CIB obtains higher accuracy at slightly higher compression (32% vs 29%) than L1-Exact, confirming that semantic cost better preserves essential reasoning.
exports.label: cib_pareto_dominates_l1

exports.content: The Conditional Information Bottleneck (CIB) framework provides a principled information-theoretic foundation for efficient LLM reasoning by: (1) identifying the Attention Paradox that invalidates standard IB for transformers, (2) deriving a semantically-grounded RL objective from CIB principles, (3) unifying existing budget-forcing methods as special cases with degenerate priors, and (4) empirically demonstrating Pareto improvements over token-count baselines across five math benchmarks.
exports.label: cib_principled_framework

exports.content: The Conditional Information Bottleneck (CIB) framework resolves the Attention Paradox by treating the prompt X as side information always available to the answer generator. The CIB objective maximizes $I(Z;Y|X)$ (how much Z explains Y given X) while minimizing $I(X;Z)$ (how redundant Z is with X). This correctly accounts for the transformer's direct access to X during answer generation.
exports.label: cib_resolves_paradox

exports.content: The CIB framework unifies all budget-forcing methods as special cases: length penalties correspond to CIB with uniform priors (Proposition 4.1), and target-length penalties correspond to CIB with Laplace priors (Proposition 4.2). Semantic priors (from a real language model $Q_\phi$) strictly generalize these, capturing token-level relevance that length-only metrics cannot.
exports.label: cib_unifies_budget_forcing

exports.content:
On DeepScaleR-1.5B with CIB using a larger 7B prior model, average accuracy decreases from 44.7% (baseline) to 44.0% (−0.7pp), while average token count decreases from 6,722 to 3,951 tokens (−41%). This demonstrates aggressive compression with minimal accuracy cost:

| Benchmark | Acc (base) | Acc (CIB-7B) | Tok (base) | Tok (CIB-7B) |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| MATH500   | 85.8%     | 84.0%       | 3,190     | 1,548       |
| AIME24    | 38.1%     | 35.2%       | 9,269     | 5,720       |
| AIME25    | 25.2%     | 25.8%       | 8,901     | 5,362       |
| Minerva   | 19.9%     | 19.6%       | 6,375     | 3,097       |
| Olympiad  | 54.7%     | 55.2%       | 5,875     | 4,029       |
exports.label: deepscaler_7b_prior

exports.content:
On DeepScaleR-1.5B with CIB using 1.5B prior ($\beta^+ = 1.5\times10^{-4}$, aggressive), average accuracy improves from 44.7% (baseline) to 46.0% (+1.3pp), while average token count decreases from 6,722 (baseline) to 4,797 tokens (−29%):

| Benchmark | Acc (base) | Acc (CIB) | Tok (base) | Tok (CIB) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| MATH500   | 85.8%     | 87.8%     | 3,190     | 2,106    |
| AIME24    | 38.1%     | 40.2%     | 9,269     | 6,857    |
| AIME25    | 25.2%     | 26.3%     | 8,901     | 6,672    |
| Minerva   | 19.9%     | 20.3%     | 6,375     | 4,144    |
| Olympiad  | 54.7%     | 55.3%     | 5,875     | 4,208    |
exports.label: deepscaler_cib_aggressive

exports.content:
On DeepScaleR-1.5B with CIB using 1.5B prior ($\beta^- = 5\times10^{-5}$, conservative), average accuracy improves from 44.7% (baseline) to 45.9% (+1.2pp), while average token count decreases from 6,722 (baseline) to 5,045 tokens (−25%):

| Benchmark | Acc (base) | Acc (CIB) | Tok (base) | Tok (CIB) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| MATH500   | 85.8%     | 87.6%     | 3,190     | 2,359    |
| AIME24    | 38.1%     | 39.2%     | 9,269     | 7,156    |
| AIME25    | 25.2%     | 27.3%     | 8,901     | 6,725    |
| Minerva   | 19.9%     | 20.1%     | 6,375     | 4,512    |
| Olympiad  | 54.7%     | 55.4%     | 5,875     | 4,473    |
exports.label: deepscaler_cib_conservative

exports.content:
On DLER-7B with CIB using 7B prior ($\beta^+ = 1.5\times10^{-4}$, aggressive), average accuracy decreases from 54.2% (baseline) to 52.9% (−1.3pp), while average token count decreases from 2,481 (baseline) to 1,687 tokens (−32%):

| Benchmark | Acc (base) | Acc (CIB) | Tok (base) | Tok (CIB) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| MATH500   | 93.6%     | 92.2%     | 1,159     | 678      |
| AIME24    | 48.5%     | 48.3%     | 3,261     | 2,617    |
| AIME25    | 36.9%     | 35.6%     | 3,264     | 2,580    |
| Minerva   | 27.2%     | 25.8%     | 1,872     | 849      |
| Olympiad  | 65.0%     | 62.6%     | 2,851     | 1,711    |
exports.label: dler_7b_cib_aggressive

exports.content:
On DLER-7B with CIB using 7B prior ($\beta^- = 5\times10^{-5}$, conservative), average accuracy improves from 54.2% (baseline) to 54.3% (+0.1pp), while average token count decreases from 2,481 (baseline) to 2,285 tokens (−8%):

| Benchmark | Acc (base) | Acc (CIB) | Tok (base) | Tok (CIB) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| MATH500   | 93.6%     | 94.0%     | 1,159     | 1,033    |
| AIME24    | 48.5%     | 49.4%     | 3,261     | 3,003    |
| AIME25    | 36.9%     | 37.1%     | 3,264     | 3,113    |
| Minerva   | 27.2%     | 26.7%     | 1,872     | 1,643    |
| Olympiad  | 65.0%     | 64.3%     | 2,851     | 2,632    |
exports.label: dler_7b_cib_conservative

exports.content: The CIB framework is general and instantiable with different verifiers $Q_\rho$ and priors $Q_\phi$ for task-specific optimization. While demonstrated on mathematical reasoning with binary correctness verifiers, the objective $R(X,Y,Z) = r_{\text{acc}} + \beta r_{\text{min}}$ can accommodate other domains with appropriate verifier designs.
exports.label: framework_generality

exports.content: CIB training requires a frozen base language model $Q_\phi$ during training but does not add any overhead at inference time. The trained policy $\pi_\theta$ generates compressed reasoning traces autonomously without needing to query $Q_\phi$.
exports.label: no_inference_overhead

exports.content: Proposition 4.1: A standard length-based penalty $g(Z) = \alpha f(|Z|)$ (applied in existing Budget Forcing methods) is equivalent to the CIB minimality reward $r_{\text{min}}(X,Z) = \sum_t \log Q_\phi(z_t|z_{<t})$ under the special case where $Q_\phi$ is a maximum entropy (uniform) prior over the vocabulary. Under this prior, $-\log Q_\phi(z_t|z_{<t}) = \log|V|$ is constant per token, so $C(Z) \propto |Z|$.
exports.label: prop_length_penalty_equivalence

exports.content: Proposition 4.2: Target-length penalties, such as L1-Exact (LCPO-Exact) [@Aggarwal2025], which penalize deviation from a target length $L^*$, correspond to the CIB objective with a Laplace prior $Q_\phi$ peaked at $L^*$. Under a Laplace prior, $\log Q_\phi(Z) \approx -\lambda ||Z| - L^*|$, matching the L1 penalty form.
exports.label: prop_target_length_equivalence

exports.content: The CIB framework yields a principled reinforcement learning training objective: maximize task reward while compressing completions under a prior over reasoning traces. Concretely, the policy $\pi_\theta$ is trained using GRPO [@Shao2024] with the combined reward $R(X,Y,Z) = r_{\text{acc}}(X,Y,Z) + \beta \cdot r_{\text{min}}(X,Z)$, where $\beta$ is the compression trade-off hyperparameter.
exports.label: rl_objective_formulation
